front: Ruler: sixth Mughal emperor, successor of his father Shah Jahan
Ruler: known as "Alamgir" ("world-seizer") for bringing the Mughal Empire to its greatest territorial extent
Ruler: tons of his treasure were stored on the armed ship Ganj-i-Sawai, which was captured by the pirate Henry Avery
Event: his ascent to power was completed with victory at the Battle of Samugarh, after which he was crowned emperor
Event: imprisoned/confined his father Shah Jahan in Agra Fort
Event: after winning the civil war, accused his brother Dara Shikoh of heresy and executed him
Event: convinced his youngest brother Murad Baksh to stake claim to the throne, ostensibly supporting him, then defeated their eldest brother at the Battle of Deorai
Event: warred for the throne with his brothers Shah Shuja and Dara Shikoh
Ruler: to atone for his violent rise to power over his siblings, married his son to his nephew's daughter Salima Begum and his own daughters to his nephews Sipihr Shikoh and Izid Bakhsh
Law: reinstated the jizya tax on non-Muslims, which had previously been abolished by Akbar
Document: a compendium of Hanafi Islamic jurisprudence compiled by almost 500 jurists at his order
Law: his farmans banned syncretic court customs like Nowruz and jharokha darshan, ending the practice of addressing subjects from an ornate balcony
Law: banned the printing of the kalima (Islamic profession of faith) on coins
Ruler: prosecuted the mystic Sarmad Kashani for repeatedly claiming "There is no God"
Ruler: destroyed many Hindu temples during his reign, including building the Shahi Eidgah in Mathura on the site of a previous temple in response to the Jat Rebellion
Ruler: his policies were opposed by Raja Ram, who defiled the grave of a previous emperor during his rebellion
Ruler: executed the Sikh Guru Tegh Bahadur for refusing to convert to Islam
Document: an "epistle of victory" denouncing Aurangzeb, written by Guru Gobind Singh after the Battle of Chamkaur
Ruler: a letter addressed to him declares "I have no trust in your oaths anymore," written by Gobind Singh after escaping Aurangzeb's forces at Chamkaur
Event: his first major battle was the Siege of Orchha (1635), part of the Bundela War; his father gave him a sword afterward from which he took his regnal name
Ruler: greatly expanded his empire through the Ahom Campaign and a series of wars against the sultanates of the Deccan
Ruler: his general Shaista Khan conquered Chittagong from the Portuguese and Arakanese pirates during his reign
Event: the Mughals fought Mongol-khanate-backed Tibetan forces at the Battle of Basgo during his reign
Ruler: chief military rival of the Maratha ruler Shivaji, who fought primarily against his forces
Ruler: Shivaji became his vassal (per the Treaty of Purandar), and he then placed Shivaji under house arrest at his court in Agra
Ruler: beheaded/executed the Maratha ruler Sambhaji, Shivaji's son and successor
Ruler: abandoned the pluralist religious policies of his father Shah Jahan
Ruler: his military successes overextended the Mughal Empire and placed hardship on the peasants who funded his exploits
Ruler: the Mughal Empire's decline is often traced back to his death in 1707, though the empire was not fully deposed until 1857
Ruler: Gujarat fell into chaos after his death in 1707
Work: debut novel that contains an allegory about Aurangzeb's trial of his brother Dara Shikoh
